title: The Art of Digital Typography
subtitle: On the craft of setting type by algorithm — Knuth-Plass line breaking, Unicode, and the pursuit of even word spacing
leftColumn: Typography is the art of arranging type to make written language legible, readable, and appealing when displayed. The arrangement of type involves selecting typefaces, point sizes, line lengths, line spacing, and letter spacing, as well as adjusting the space between pairs of letters. But of all the decisions a typographer makes, none is more fundamental than how words are distributed across lines. A single paragraph of justified text contains dozens of invisible choices: where to break each line, how much to stretch or compress each word space, whether to hyphenate a long word or carry it whole to the next line. These decisions compound. A poor break on line three forces awkward spacing on line four, which pushes a widow to the top of the next column. The greedy algorithm used by most software makes each decision in isolation, filling one line at a time without considering the consequences downstream.

In 1981, Donald Knuth and Michael Plass published a paper that changed how computers set type. Their algorithm, implemented in the TeX typesetting system, considers an entire paragraph at once. Instead of greedily filling each line, it evaluates every possible combination of line breaks and selects the one that minimizes a global measure of ugliness — a quantity they called "total demerits." The result is paragraphs with remarkably even word spacing, free of the rivers of whitespace that plague greedy justification. The algorithm models text as a sequence of boxes, glue, and penalties: boxes are rigid content like word fragments, glue is stretchable and shrinkable space, and penalties represent the cost of breaking at a given point. Hyphenation points carry a positive penalty, discouraging but not prohibiting breaks within words. The dynamic programming solution runs in time proportional to the number of feasible breakpoints, which in practice is fast enough to be imperceptible.

The difference between greedy and optimal line breaking is most visible in narrow columns. When the line length is short relative to average word length, the greedy algorithm frequently produces lines with just two or three words stretched across the full measure, creating distracting gaps. The Knuth-Plass algorithm avoids this by accepting slightly tighter spacing on one line to prevent extreme looseness on the next. It assigns each line a "fitness class" — tight, normal, loose, or very loose — and penalizes adjacent lines whose fitness classes differ by more than one step. This penalty, combined with the cubic badness function that makes extreme ratios exponentially expensive, produces the even texture that characterizes professionally typeset documents. The human eye is remarkably sensitive to variations in word spacing; we perceive uneven spacing as a flaw even before we consciously notice it.
rightColumnIntro: Unicode introduced a further dimension to the problem. The line-breaking rules that work for English — break at spaces, hyphens, and after certain punctuation — fail for scripts without explicit word boundaries. Chinese, Japanese, and Korean text can break between almost any two characters, but not before certain punctuation marks or after opening brackets. The Unicode Line Break Algorithm, defined in UAX #14, codifies these rules for every character in the Unicode standard. A modern text layout engine must consult these rules at every character position, transforming the simple question "can I break here?" into a lookup against a database of character properties, pair tables, and contextual rules. When combined with the Knuth-Plass algorithm, these break opportunities become penalty items in the optimization, allowing the engine to find optimal breaks across any script or language.

pullQuote.text: "Typography exists to honor content. Good typography lets the reader focus on the message, not the medium."
pullQuote.attribution: Robert Bringhurst, The Elements of Typographic Style

sections.heading: Schriftsatz und Druckgeschichte
sections.body: Die Geschichte des Schriftsatzes ist untrennbar mit der Entwicklung des Buchdrucks verbunden. Johannes Gutenberg erfand um 1440 in Mainz den Druck mit beweglichen Lettern und revolutionierte damit die Verbreitung von Wissen in Europa. Jeder einzelne Buchstabe wurde als Metalltype gegossen und von Hand in den Setzkasten eingeordnet. Der Schriftsetzer stand am Setzregal und griff Buchstabe für Buchstabe aus den Fächern, fügte Wortzwischenräume aus Blindmaterial ein und achtete darauf, dass jede Zeile exakt auf die Satzbreite ausgeschlossen war. Dieses Ausschließen — das gleichmäßige Verteilen des überschüssigen Raums auf die Wortzwischenräume einer Zeile — ist im Kern dasselbe Problem, das der Knuth-Plass-Algorithmus heute auf digitalem Weg löst.

sections.heading: La tradition typographique française
sections.body: La typographie française possède ses propres conventions, héritées de plusieurs siècles de tradition imprimée. Les espaces insécables avant les signes de ponctuation doubles, la finesse des caractères Garamond et Didot, et le soin apporté à la césure des mots témoignent d'une culture où la mise en page est considérée comme un art à part entière.
